title: College Admissions Accountability Act of 2025
plain_summary: The College Admissions Accountability Act of 2025 creates a new government office to watch over how colleges choose their students. This office will look into complaints that colleges are unfairly picking students based on their race or other things that break the law. If colleges are found breaking civil rights rules, they could lose money from the government. The goal is to make sure all students are treated fairly when applying to college.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the College Admissions Accountability Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts. First, in the twenty-first century, getting into top colleges works like a sorting machine that decides who gets prestige and good opportunities by giving out a limited number of valuable degrees. Second, for many decades, colleges and universities in the United States used admission rules and practices that gave special preferences to students based on their race. Third, in a court case called Students for Fair Admissions versus Harvard College in two thousand twenty-three, the Supreme Court of the United States decided that admissions policies based on race violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to the Constitution. The Court also said that universities cannot simply use application essays or other methods to get around this ruling. Fourth, Title Six of the Civil Rights Act of nineteen sixty-four says that organizations that receive federal money cannot discriminate based on race. Fifth, after the Supreme Court made its ruling in the Harvard case, several colleges and universities made statements or created new policies that go against what the Court decided. Sixth, colleges and universities, including their admissions offices, must follow the Constitution and laws of the United States as explained by the courts. Seventh, it is the job of the United States Government to protect the civil rights of its citizens and to make sure the Constitution and laws are followed as the courts explain them.

sections.title: Section 3 Special Inspector General for Unlawful Discrimination in Higher Education
sections.text: This section adds a new part to the Department of Education Organization Act. It creates an Office of the Special Inspector General for Unlawful Discrimination in Higher Education. The office defines important terms. Appropriate committees of Congress means specific committees in the Senate and House that deal with education and funding. A covered individual means any person who applies to or is enrolled as a student at a covered institution. A covered institution means any college or university that receives federal student aid money or other federal institutional aid. The section establishes the Office of the Special Inspector General within the Department of Education. The head of this office is called the Special Inspector General for Unlawful Discrimination in Higher Education. The President appoints this person with approval from the Senate. The person chosen should have integrity and know about higher education and admissions, plus have skills in auditing, civil rights, law, academic administration, education rules, or investigations. The Special Inspector General can be removed from office following specific federal rules. Their pay will be the same as other Inspector Generals. The Special Inspector General has several duties. They must receive, review, and investigate complaints from students or college employees about admissions decisions, policies, practices, financial aid decisions, or academic programs that violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment based on the Harvard court case or Title Six of the Civil Rights Act. They must review any federal policies and programs that encourage colleges to violate these laws. They must make recommendations to the college, the Secretary of Education, the Attorney General, and Congress about how colleges should fix violations, what employees should face discipline, whether colleges should keep getting federal money, whether more investigation is needed, and how to reform federal policies that encourage violations. The Special Inspector General must set up proper systems and procedures to do their job. They also have all the regular duties and responsibilities that other inspector generals have under federal law. They must keep secret the identity of anyone who gives them information. The Special Inspector General has the same powers and authorities as other inspector generals under federal law. Their office is considered the same type of office as other inspector general offices. The Special Inspector General can hire officers and employees needed to do their work. These positions are schedule C positions in federal regulations. They can hire experts and consultants at daily rates not higher than the equivalent of grade GS-fifteen of the General Schedule. They can make contracts with public agencies and private people for audits, studies, analyses, and other services and pay what is necessary to do their job. The Special Inspector General must write reports every three months starting within sixty days of being confirmed. These reports must summarize their activities during the previous three months. Each report must include a general description of complaints received and reviewed, recommendations for reforms, steps that colleges have taken or still need to take to fix problems, data about the number of complaints and violations found broken down by college and including separate information about racial bias, and comments about how well each college has cooperated with investigations and whether they have followed recommendations. The law says nothing in this section allows public disclosure of information that is prohibited by other laws, required to be protected by Executive order for national defense or security or foreign affairs, or part of an ongoing criminal investigation. Twenty-five million dollars is authorized to be spent to carry out this section, and this money stays available until it is used. The Special Inspector General will be a member of the Council of the Inspectors General on Integrity and Efficiency. Colleges must take action to fix problems identified by the Special Inspector General or certify to the Special Inspector General and Congress that no action is needed. Nothing in this section limits the authority of the regular Inspector General of the Department of Education. The Office of the Special Inspector General will end twelve years after this law is passed. The section also updates the table of contents of the Department of Education Organization Act to include the new section. Finally, the section adds a new part to the Higher Education Act of nineteen sixty-five. It says that no college or university can receive federal student aid or other federal institutional aid money if the Secretary of Education determines that the institution had admissions decisions, policies, practices, financial aid decisions, or academic programs that discriminated based on race in violation of the Equal Protection Clause based on the Harvard court case or Title Six of the Civil Rights Act.
